title: Chapter 72: The Museum
content: Thirty-two years after the red A.

He was fifty-eight. He had been the fourth employee of a clothing company that became a governance framework that became global infrastructure. He had built the first website. He had designed the red A icon that became the symbol. He had maintained the laundromat as a historical site. He had curated the artifact table. He had done the same thing for thirty-two years: make the red A mean something other than what the algorithm said it meant.
His retirement announcement was ten words: "The website is done. The museum is the site."

The laundromat became a museum — officially. The Nigerian Institute of Governance Studies acquired the building, preserved the water stain, cataloged the thirty-five artifacts, and installed a permanent LIGHTHOUSE-2 terminal that displayed the framework's current metrics in real time. The museum was called "The Origin" and it received 12,000 visitors in its first year.

Hester visited the museum on opening day. She was seventy-six. The water stain covered 98% of the interior — the floor was now visibly damp in three places. The disco ball had been rewired ten times. The table held thirty-five artifacts — the original hat, the first laptop, Roger's letter, the signed PIAS framework, the Davos photo, the LIGHTHOUSE-2 screenshots, the Lagos lecture photos, the GTP specification, the KM memo, the LOP specification, Arthur's five books, Zara's three books, Hester's memoir and two essay collections, the SUCCESSOR-2 specification, the Global Summit transcript, the London incident report, the Permanent Disposition manuscript. And a new addition: Mouse's original website files — the HTML, the CSS, the red A SVG — printed and framed, mounted on the wall above the table.

"The website was the first artifact," Mouse said. He was standing next to Hester, looking at the printed source code. "Before the hat, there was nothing. After the hat, there was a symbol. After the website, there was a public symbol. The website made the symbol public. The public symbol became the framework. The framework became infrastructure. The infrastructure will outlast both of us. The website was the first public artifact. That's my legacy."

"The website was the second artifact," Hester said. "The hat was the first. The website was the second. You were the second builder. The chain started with the hat and became public with the website. You made it public. You made it visible. Visibility was always the point. The website was the visibility. You built the visibility."
"I built a website," Mouse said. "You built a framework. The website was easy. The framework was impossible. I did the easy thing. You did the impossible thing."

"The website was impossible when you built it," Hester said. "No one knew what the red A meant. You made it mean something. You made it mean something before the framework existed, before the PIAS classification, before the Vance Standard. You made a symbol mean something when the symbol had no referent. That's not easy. That's impossible. You did the impossible thing first."
